ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cheese-foam-topped peach tea. FOODON_03311069 (tea beverage) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000 (Tea and tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for cheese-foam tea drinks exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 74242007 (Tea) used as closest parent; no specific concept for cheese mousse tea beverage exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 500 ml (500 g), equivalent to a large takeaway cup as typically served at Taiwanese-style tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy 14355) combined with component estimation for cheese foam topping using USDA FoodData Central cream cheese and heavy cream entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for peach cheese mousse tea exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by component modelling: brewed black tea base (~85% by weight, USDA SR Legacy 14355), peach syrup contribution (~8% by weight, estimated from USDA FoodData Central peach nectar/syrup analogue), and cheese mousse topping (~7% by weight, estimated from USDA cream cheese FDC 173417 and heavy cream FDC 170859). Per-100g values represent the blended beverage as consumed. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweetened tea beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peach cheese mousse tea. GI of 55 estimated by analogy with sweetened tea beverages and fruit-flavoured drinks documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The cheese mousse topping contributes fat and protein which moderately suppress glycaemic response; the dominant glycaemic driver is the peach syrup and sugar content. GI category is low per convention for values 55 or less. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 39.0) / 100 ≈ 21.5, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 7.8 × 5 = 39.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and added sugar load
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 ml serving contributes approximately 140 mg sodium and a substantial sugar load; individuals managing hypertension should limit consumption to occasional treats and avoid adding extra sweeteners.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content and moderate glycaemic load despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated low glycaemic index (55) but a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 21.5 per serving due to 39 g of carbohydrate predominantly from added peach syrup and sugar, this beverage can still pose a significant postprandial glucose challenge and should be consumed with caution or avoided by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese mousse topping and high sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cheese mousse topping contributes approximately 10 g of saturated fat per serving, and the high sugar content may contribute to dyslipidaemia; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and prefer unsweetened tea alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peach Tea with Cheese Mousse (蜜桃奶盖茶) is a Taiwanese-origin specialty tea beverage that has become widely popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia, particularly through specialty tea chains such as CHICHA San Chen. The drink consists of a chilled or iced peach-flavoured tea base — typically brewed black tea or oolong tea infused or blended with peach syrup or peach puree — topped with a thick, lightly salted whipped cheese mousse (奶盖, nǎi gài) made from cream cheese, heavy cream, milk, and sugar. The beverage is consumed by drinking through the cheese foam layer, which creates a contrast between the sweet-fruity tea and the rich, creamy, slightly savoury topping. The texture is smooth and indulgent, with a pronounced peach aroma and a milky finish. Nutritionally, a standard 500 ml serving provides approximately 310 kcal, 39 g of carbohydrate (predominantly from added sugar), 16 g of fat (including approximately 10 g saturated fat from the dairy mousse), and 4.5 g of protein. The beverage has a low glycaemic index (55) but a moderate glycaemic load (~21.5 per serving) driven by the peach syrup and sugar content. Clinically, this drink is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar load, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat content from the cheese mousse. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan or dairy-free. It is best treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular beverage choice for health-conscious consumers.

Peach tea with mousse nutrition facts

confidence_reason: No direct entry for peach cheese mousse tea exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Values were estimated by component modelling using USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed black tea, peach syrup analogue, cream cheese, and heavy cream. GI was estimated by analogy with sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported in USDA FoodData Central and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries; no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific beverage. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace per USDA cream cheese and heavy cream entries — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation model and cannot be defensibly summed without direct measurement. GI set to 55 as an analogous estimate for sweetened tea beverages per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category low per convention for GI values at or below 55. GL per serving = (55 × 39.0) / 100 = 21.45, rounded to 21.5. Serving size of 500 g used as standard large cup size consistent with Taiwanese tea chain portion norms in Singapore. Halal status assumed based on ingredients; individual restaurant certification should be verified. The food_name in the input record was 'Peach Tea with Mousse蜜桃奶盖'; canonical English name normalised to 'Peach Tea with Cheese Mousse' for precision. Chinese name from input record (蜜桃奶盖) preserved and expanded to full form 蜜桃奶盖茶.
